segments.text: I've got a really interesting expert with me today what do you think you would know about aesthetic careers if you talk to 10,000 people about their hopes and dreams in the field of Aesthetics so on this episode we're going to unpack many of the things that he's learned about Medics trying to venture out into the world of Aesthetics to help you accelerate your journey because if there's one thing I've learned over the years is that although it often feels like your battles are just yours they're actually really similar across the board we all go through different stages hit similar obstacles sometimes with different names but they're basically the same thing mindset training and the right steps to take in order to advance your career and Jay is going to help us do that a lot faster with his wealth of experience so welcome Jay hi Tim thanks for having me today we've worked together for years now and um I always love listening to what you have to say about the people that you've been talking to on the phone because and there's something I've also learned through my own experiences there's no replacing experience so when you've had 10,000 interactions with someone with a group of people you

understand the patterns that other people don't see and that's what I'm excited to kind of get from you today if we can start with that one what do you think when someone calls up and asks for advice on training on what to do and how to develop their careers what do you think the most common situation is that they're in at that point in their lives I speak to people from all different backgrounds um from all different medical professions people are in different stages of their Journey some PE some people are really interested or intrigued about Aesthetics so some people I speak to who are at the beginning of the journey never injected in the face before and they're really intrigued or interested about what this Venture can lead to um other people I'll speak to they'll have done their job for 5 years 10 years they're a little bit sick of their day today monotony of doing the same thing day in day out so it just really depends on each individual so I speak to individuals like I said from all different medical backgrounds it's my job to then have a comprehensive consultation with those individual uals uh to really get

to the bottom of what it is that's making them want to do this some people may it may just be a passing interest um and they just wanted more a bit more information on what this can lead to other people really want a way out and potentially look at a new vocation to commit all their kind of time and effort and full Endeavors towards I remember when I first started uh looked into training my trigger was being in a job where I could not negotiate my annual leave at all basically I thought I was going to work in hospital my whole career and I had various other financial reasons cuz they do this weird thing with in the UK with banding on doctor's jobs so you get you apply for a job you think it's going to be on like whatever 30 grand a year and then you find out your job to isn't banded and you're actually on 21 Grand yeah but you bought a house on 31 so now you have to figure out how you're going to basically make a bit more income um that's what that was one of the triggers but the real thing um that really tipped me into it more was the lack of autonomy so I remember working in

accident emergency trying to get back to South Africa for the first time since I immigrated and uh really desperate to go and I just could not negotiate the leave they just wouldn't eventually I did it by swapping like I got two other people to swap and I swapped with them but it was so annoying and I thinking I was going to be the only one in my family not to go back for the first time that I thought I need something where I can have a bit more autonomy and then my friend told me about Aesthetics and I was like I'm going for that so I would say was from a position of pain but then later on discovering that I really enjoyed it as well so it wasn't actually the first trigger I think some people might benefit from hearing that because if you haven't fallen in love with it but you're interested it you know sometimes that comes later as well do you find most people are they motivated towards something or are they trying to move away from something I think there's a combination to be honest quite often I'll speak to people and they will have as well as obviously working as a doctor or a dentist or a nurse or whatever medical profession it may be they'll obviously have to juggle their family life their children any

extracurricular activities hobbies and quite like yourself they've just not had the luxury of time so they'll have to try and juggle everything and like quite rightly said they don't have any control over things like annual leave or when they can finish or when they can't finish um missing really important moments of life watching the kids go to school grow up recital and everything else really the about Aesthetics it really does give you the autonomy to pick and choose the hours was ultimately your investing time and effort uh and finances in yourself in essentially a business so like I said by doing so you do have the opportunity to take a lot more control over your life and that work life balance so quite often I'll speak to people who are young moms for example and they just don't have that flexibility because they'll work for the NHS or whoever it may be more often than not I speak to

NHS employees they'll have X amount of time off after maternity leave and then they'll know they have to go back to it not everybody can afford to take a two three four year how many of a year Gap um to kind of spend and enjoy that pressure time with their children so they'll have to kind of be forced to go back whereas with Aesthetics you can work around everything like that so if you have young children and you're struggling with time then the beauty about it is you do have that flexibility and autonomy and the ability to pick and choose when you do that whereas that just isn't possible when in employed work I have a great story about that actually one of our earliest trainees and I always you obviously as we train more and more people it it's harder to remember them all but um Sarah Murphy you trained with me one of the earliest trainees and she set up a clinic called anti-aging Aesthetics and uh I always remember her because she was so heavily pregnant on her first training day and I remember thinking because we had a one-year-old at that point and we were in that breaking point of like gosh this

is hard having and thinking that's going to be really hard work for her to build a practice while she's having kids and all that stuff and lo and behold she's done so well she's got a thriving practice you know loads of five star reviews and employees loads of Staff um she's doing so well and it just goes I always like that story because I think what I like about it is that she overcame what I what my objections were like overcame what I thought was a difficult situation and just absolutely nailed it and it made me think differently about what's possible um and there's so many cases like that and it it's actually largely I'd say was like 80% female nurses um and they go out and do things that I don't think maybe even they themselves think are possible when they first start and that was my experience as well I remember my goal at the beginning I'd like to hear what people say their goals are I'm always interested in people's goals but my goal at the beginning was to work a little bit on Saturdays and Sundays and supplement my income and now it's my whole life so you never know but do how

many people um do you think have clear goals about what they want to achieve or are clear to you know on the phone about whether they really want a career or whether they just want some sort of side hustle honestly it's actually a very small percentage who will have actually a set goal in mind I kind of take it upon myself to ask a series of questions to get a comprehensive understanding of what it is they want to do quite often people get in touch and they'll say um Jay tell me a little bit about the courses or they'll sometimes they'll even get in touch and say oh I want to do X Y and Z course um and then I take it upon myself to ask a series of questions to try and get to the ultimate goal and then that will allow me to then carve out a bit of a path for them to go down to help them achieve that sometimes when asking them questions I think they realize that actually what they thought they initially wanted to do isn't actually the case and just highlighting that quite often gives them a little bit of a different perspective and then even sometimes it allows them

to kind of go back to the drawing board and think rethink what it is they actually want from this just like you touched upon before just a briefly kind of mention ult ultimately even one of the clinicians over at skin via Training Academy Shon Middleton for example I remember when she newly joined she actually was still in the NHS and although we all loved NHS sadly we know the absolute dedication the hard work the Blood Sweat and Tears that our NHS doctors and nurses and everybody else involved are putting in sadly they're just not rewarded as they should be and quite often I will speak to clinicians and they will quite often I'll ask them what it is they why they wanting to do it and they'll tell me it's because of time or autonomy and then they'll kind of dance a little bit around the fact that it's to make a little bit of extra money and I always say to everybody please don't ever feel ashamed or embarrassed about wanting to make a good living for yourself because firstly it's not going to be easy you're going to have to put in a hell of a lot of work to actually become not

that this particular vocation has given her the opportunity to pick and choose what it is she wants to do and devote more time and effort in something that she actually loves as opposed to doing something where she may not be getting the financial reimbursement that she actually deserves from the NHS so like I said touching upon again on the NHS we do quite often get a lot of people who are putting in a 110% and just not getting anything close to what they could achieve financially by doing Aesthetics yeah I always think of the um you know the there's this human needs list and we basically all humans have the same needs we meet the needs in different ways but I think the healthc care is full of people who basically want to heal other people they want the Delight of seeing someone feel better you know it's that six-year-old girl who wants to put the plaster on her mom's knee or sister's knee to play a role in them getting better um and then to get the credit for that as well so a little bit of qos you

know being respected for your job um having connection with patients feeling like you're contributing something that matters um and then also having your financial needs met because you do an important job that's basically what being a medic was supposed to be but because of the nature of we've got we're a difficult situation with an aging population uh not training enough people and there's not enough money in our Healthcare System so that all gets shunted further and further down the list until you're basically firefighting you're unimportant in your role because you know you don't feel like you can change anything um everyone's short of resources often in teams in the NHS stress teams don't always treat each other nicely so you have this a lot of nurses complain about kind of bullying and all that kind of stuff that happens in the NHS plus you're not getting compensated for the effort and the time and the night shift and all that stuff and I think it's really important that people understand that you're not necessarily doing a disservice to the NHS by saying I've got something better to do because that will make the NHS have to think more about how they fund it and how many staff they have and

um and also I mean I still work at the NHS one day a week but it's it I do it on my terms and I think that's one of the great things about Aesthetics is you can start to like Sharon did for so long you can do both for a while if you want to and then you can move in and I found that a much easier transition than just resigning all of a sudden so um but I also want people to feel legitimate if they're if in their unhappiness because it's normal to feel a bit pissed off with your job when it wasn't what you pictured it being and you know you're it's just not meeting your needs and these are just needs they're not negotiable things they're not likes you have to have them or you're not going to be happy so to go after that is 100% legitimate and a good thing to do for everyone because we need productive happy people in Society not crushed people who are you know about to go on the sick for six months because their job is so awful so um anyway that's my little rant about going after your happiness and not letting other people because a lot of people have friction over their identity and
